summary: Beans fix atmospheric nitrogen that benefits heavy-feeding corn; corn stalks act as a trellis for climbing beans. Core of the Three Sisters; yield benefits are real but vary by variety and spacing.
Squash's sprawling leaves shade the soil, suppress weeds and retain moisture around corn.
Grown together in the Three Sisters; pairwise benefit is plausible (weed suppression, some N) but less directly studied than the full trio.
Popular pairing; basil may deter some pests and attract pollinators, but rigorous evidence for improved tomato yield or flavor is limited. Traditional, plausible.
Black walnut roots, hulls and leaves release juglone, a documented respiration inhibitor; tomatoes are highly sensitive (wilting, death). Keep tomatoes well outside the root zone.
Tagetes suppress root-knot nematodes via alpha-terthienyl; strongest when grown as a dense planting or rotation the prior season, not just a few interplanted flowers.
Widely repeated claim that alliums stunt legumes; little rigorous evidence. Folklore — shown only with the tradition toggle.
Nasturtium used as an aphid / squash-bug trap crop to draw pests off cucurbits; commonly recommended, limited controlled data.
White clover as orchard living mulch fixes nitrogen, retains moisture, suppresses weeds and feeds pollinators beneath apple.
Comfrey is a classic fruit-tree-guild chop-and-drop mulch; deep taproot is claimed to mine nutrients. Benefit plausible but 'dynamic accumulation' is not well quantified.
Native wild strawberry as edible living mulch under apple: suppresses weeds, holds moisture, feeds pollinators. Guild tradition.
Wild bergamot draws pollinators and predatory insects into the apple guild; pairwise benefit plausible, not quantified.
Purple coneflower pulls pollinators and beneficial insects toward the apple guild; traditional, unquantified.
Horseradish at the drip line is a classic fruit-tree-guild pest/disease deterrent claim; traditional, unproven.
Groundnut is an N-fixing native vine; scrambling at a persimmon's base can add nitrogen. Keep it off young trunks — it twines.
Wild strawberry groundcover beneath a pawpaw; both tolerate part shade. Guild tradition.
N-fixing groundnut alongside elderberry; both like moist ground. Plausible, untested pairwise.
Wild strawberry as living mulch under blueberry; pH needs overlap on the acidic side. Guild tradition.
Mulberry ripens with blueberries and lures birds away as a decoy crop; widely recommended, little controlled data.
Apple/crabapple are listed by extensions as juglone-susceptible; keep apple well outside a black walnut's root zone. Lists are observational.
Brambles (Rubus) are reported juglone-susceptible; separate from black walnut.
Blueberry is reported juglone-susceptible; keep it away from black walnut.
Both tall, aggressive heavy feeders; sunchoke is also allelopathic and crowds neighbors. Give them separate ground.
A mature oak's canopy provides the partial shade young pawpaw needs; but oak's deep shade and dry, root-filled soil slow establishment — expect to irrigate.
American hazelnut tolerates the partial shade at an oak's edge; a way to use an existing shade tree productively.
Silver maple's shallow, aggressive roots and dense dry shade outcompete blueberry for water and nutrients; poor spot to underplant.
Even a groundcover struggles in silver maple's dry, root-filled zone; the canopy also sheds rain away from underplantings.
Eastern red cedar is the alternate host for cedar-apple rust; apples within ~1–3 miles get infected. Separate them or plant rust-resistant apple cultivars.
Pine as an acidic-litter neighbor/windbreak for acid-loving blueberry is popular, but pine needles shift soil pH far less than folklore claims — mild effect at best.
Honey locust casts light, dappled shade well-suited to understory pawpaw. (Its reputed nitrogen fixation is unreliable — value here is the shade, not N.)
Dappled honey-locust shade lets serviceberry fruit while sheltering it; use an existing locust as a guild overstory.
Redbud is one of the earliest pollinator resources; sited near apple it can lift early-season pollinator presence for fruit set.
White clover as a living mulch under persimmon fixes nitrogen, holds moisture, and feeds pollinators. Guild tradition.
Comfrey as chop-and-drop mulch around pawpaw; deep taproot claimed to lift nutrients. Plausible, unquantified.
Wild bergamot draws pollinators that improve blueberry fruit set; native and shares acidic-to-neutral tolerance.
Comfrey chop-and-drop mulch around persimmon; deep taproot claimed to lift nutrients. Guild tradition, unquantified.
Comfrey as living mulch under mulberry; traditional guild support species.
White clover living mulch fixes nitrogen and holds moisture beneath pawpaw. Guild tradition.
Clover underplanting adds nitrogen and suppresses weeds around serviceberry.
Clover as an N-fixing living mulch at the base of hazelnut.
Clover living mulch under chokeberry; nitrogen plus weed suppression. Guild tradition.
Comfrey chop-and-drop around hazelnut; traditional nutrient-mining claim, unquantified.
Wild bergamot draws pollinators and predatory insects to the elderberry patch. Native pairing.
Monarda's pollinator draw supports serviceberry's early bloom. Traditional, plausible.
Coneflower pulls pollinators and beneficials into the elderberry guild. Native pairing.
Wild strawberry as edible living mulch under chokeberry; suppresses weeds, feeds pollinators.
Two vigorous native vines will strangle each other on one support — give them separate structures.
A vigorous grape will climb into and smother a young apple; keep grapes on their own trellis.
Bergamot adds pollinator/beneficial-insect habitat near pawpaw; note pawpaw itself is fly/beetle-pollinated, so the benefit is general habitat, not direct pollination.
Both nightshades and share late blight (Phytophthora infestans) plus Colorado potato beetle; planting together speeds disease spread. Separate and rotate 2 years.
Corn earworm and tomato fruitworm are the SAME insect (Helicoverpa zea); adjacent corn and tomato share and amplify it, especially late season.
Potato, a nightshade, is juglone-sensitive; keep it well outside a black walnut's root zone.
Fennel's root exudates (anethole/fenchone) inhibit germination and growth of many vegetables, tomato included; isolate fennel in a pot or bed edge.
Fennel allelochemicals suppress beans; a widely noted 'plant it alone' case.
Sunflower allelochemicals are notably toxic to potatoes, inhibiting sprouting and growth.
Pole beans can be stunted by sunflower allelopathy; effect is variable — worse from spent hulls than living plants.
Sunchoke (a Helianthus) is allelopathic and aggressive; keep beans clear of it.
Comfrey is juglone-tolerant — one of the few genuinely useful chop-and-drop plants that thrive under black walnut.
Pawpaw is juglone- and shade-tolerant; it can grow near/under black walnut where most fruit trees fail.
Elderberry is juglone-tolerant; safe to site near black walnut.
White clover tolerates juglone — a workable N-fixing living mulch under black walnut.
Bee balm is listed as juglone-tolerant; a pollinator option near black walnut.
Sunflowers draw pollinators that aid cucurbit fruit set, and squash tolerates sunflower allelopathy well.
Common claim that dill and fennel cross-pollinate and 'ruin' each other; they're different genera and no hybrid is documented — the real risk is only seed mix-up. Folklore.
